sentences.text: My name is Vera Eden.
I was born in Prague, Czechoslovakia, which now is the Czech Republic.
I was born on 7th of July 1930.
In Prague we lived on Vinohrady, which is-- Prague is not that big, and it is a sort of a part-- it was sort of a part of suburb.
We lived in a big apartment in a apartment building.
In our home, my father, mother, my older sister, and myself, and we had a live-in girl from a farm whom I remember very fondly because she apparently was hired when I was born.
My father's name was Anthony.
My name-- my mother's name was Francesca.
Her maiden name was Peresles.
My father was a representative of a textile firm in Prague, and he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] traveling a lot.
He had a car even in those days with a chauffeur because he did not drive.
And he was doing textile firms in the Czech Republic and taking the orders.
Whatever title it had, I couldn't tell you.
My mother was at home.
She looked after us, and she was a great mother.
My, my sister was born on the 9th of April in 1926, and she was-- she is four years older.
And she is quite the opposite of me in a way that she is very-- I'm more easygoing.
I don't know if you know the, the difference between Cancer and Aries.
There is a huge difference, and I do believe it.
And we both represent that, that category.
Oh, it was a very-- it was a very warm family life.
Though my father wasn't home, he was-- he certainly-- he left Monday mornings and came back Friday nights.
He definitely-- the quality of his time with us, even in those days, was extremely good.
And I remember him as a very gentle person to whom you can always go to if you had any problems or trouble.
He never shouted.
I have never been spanked in my life-- and neither from mother nor father.
And it was a very loving home in a very quiet, assimilated Czech family.
Oh, my mother was very pretty.
She always complained of weight, but I felt that she was very beautiful.
And she was a extremely affectionate person, and we could get away with much more in a disciplinarian way with her.
And she took us to a park, and she was very conscientious of walking us to school and from school.
And, and it was a nice environment to grow up in.
It A short, but it was beautiful.
Well, like we would have Christmas, not because of any religious reason, but because the Christmas tree was nice.
We would have Easter eggs because we could color them, you know, that kind of thing.
It-- religion actually, as far as I remember, was not a subject in our house at all.
We were taught to be truthful, honest, and I think that stayed with us for the rest of our lives-- not to cheat and not to betray.
And I think that was riveted into my brain in an early age, and it stayed with me till, till today.
Oh yes.
I was aware of being Jewish, but whatever it meant was kind of like I had friends who were Roman Catholics, but I didn't know very much about that either.
I really don't know.
I presume it must have come from the grandparents, whom I don't remember very well, and they died a natural death before the war.
And I think from there, pretty much.
In, in those days, not that I can remember.
I just had friends from school or from the park or-- I mean, that was not a question, what religion are you?
It was a question of being a nice person, and I liked it.
And therefore, I became friendly or not.
I was always very selective and pretty good in judging people on the-- on the first go.
I went to school in-- very close to where we lived.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH], if that means anything.
It doesn't really.
And it was a elementary school.
Unfortunately, in the first grade we had a teacher who shouldn't have been a teacher.
She hit the children.
And me being a kind of boisterous kid, she took more discipline towards me, and I must say that it put me off learning a great deal.
But that's besides the point, really.
Not really.
I wasn't in school that long.
Oh, it was absolutely a mixed neighborhood in, in an upper middle class environment, and I think that's about it.
We lived well.
My father-- I don't know what he made.
That, I wouldn't know, but we certainly lived extremely well.
We were taken to-- like in even in those days, we had private gymnastic lessons twice a week.
And we went to see Shirley Temple movies every week.
And my-- we were taught the German.
Funnily enough, my sister, she speaks fluent German.
She speaks more languages than I do.
But the teacher, who was a private teacher, and she used to take us for walks and teach us German.
And after about two months, she told my mother that me-- like the little one, as they used to call me-- she will never learn another language.
It's a waste of time.
My span of concentration wasn't good enough, and therefore, she advised my mother to just drop me as a pupil, which they did.
And I did learn German, but I dropped it through the war.
I do understand German very well, but to speak I think it's a matter of emotional dislike.
At home, Czech.
None.
Oh, it was great.
We had three rooms because in Europe they don't have a living room as we are used to it here.
But we had our room, my sister and myself.
Then we had a huge dining/living room with a bay window, and then my parents had their room.
Then there was a huge hall where I practice my gymnastic exercises against the wall and everything.
Then there was a big kitchen with a little balcony and, and a pantry like because in those days, you didn't have refrigerators.
And then the next to it there was a small room for the living.
Of course.
Right.
I assumed it.
Of course.
There was a full bathroom and then there was a full toilet.
Yes, in those days I wouldn't have even thought of anything else, that's why I omitted it.
Oh, just the, the, the warm, warm feeling of, of being loved, not being scared of anything, knowing that I am not-- I mean, not consciously-- but knowing that I'm being taken care of, and just loved.
The love was pure and unconditional.
Well, you know, not that I can remember.
If you would speak to my sister, she might tell you otherwise.
But as far as I know, maybe we might have lit up the Hanukkah candles because again, we are going to the pretty things, but not for religious reasons of any kind that I can remember.
Well there was a youngest brother of my father who lived close by.
I used to go and visit there because I liked his wife very much.
And she was-- she was fun, and they didn't have no children.
So I used to go and visit her, and she used to bake cookies, cookies for me, like if I didn't have enough at home.
Yes, there is one.
My mother was a very good mother, as I said.
But in the grade one, we had a vaccination-- you know smallpox vaccination-- in school.
And we had a pediatrician, of course, of course.
And my mother send a note to the principal and the teacher in the school that she does not want me to be vaccinated for smallpox at school, that I will be vaccinated privately at our pediatrician.
Then she was waiting for me at school.
This is grade one, and I remember it like today.
And we were late coming out of class and apparently-- that's what I heard and remember, funnily enough.
Mothers were waiting outside for the children, and they said, well, you know, they aren't coming out.
And one of the mothers said, oh yes, they are vaccinating them for smallpox today, so they'll be out a little later.
And my-- all I remember is the door opening in the classroom and my mother coming in like a lioness.
And I was just having the-- you know how they do the-- it's not even the injection.
It's a scratch.
And I was just having it done, and she came.
She took her clean handkerchief.
She spit on my arm, and she wrapped my arm and was very, very angry at the teacher.
And I was terribly embarrassed because I was different than the other kids.
That is one.
Not really.
Not really.
I didn't.
It was just this-- what would one call it?
Protect-- being protected.
Today maybe they would call it overprotected.
But I have the feeling that in those days, the overprotection, which I-- which I had was so nice that it sustained me all through my life.
That when you then find yourself in the world alone, and alone you are, you kind of cling to the memories of, of the protective times.
And I was lucky.
Oh, I was a very ardent lover of Prague, and it is a beautiful city, you know, structurally and historically and everything.
And everybody who goes there says what a beautiful, beautiful city, and it is.
It's got-- it's got the castle and the river running through and of course that National Theatre.
Even in those days, we were taken to it and saw operas and plays.
I mean, we were very culturally brought up, though I was a tomboy and I would try to do other things.
But culturally, we were in a theater in a-- in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't even remember.
I mean, we went to concerts.
And though I, I went and remember it very well, I don't think at that age-- I must have been very young.
We are now speaking from zero to nine, hey?
After that, it all, all finished.
But I do remember seeing the, the which is by Smetana and the Moldau concert by whoever it is by-- Dvorak is it?
I think so.
See, I can still remember that.
And that kind of thing.
When did your life begin to change?
Well, the life began to change-- it's very difficult to pinpoint.
It slowly, slowly started to-- I mean Hitler came to Austria when? 1933?
And I mean we-- well, we-- I shouldn't say we because I was a little child.
But the nervousness and apprehension what was happening in Europe, obviously, was talked about.
And Europeans as a whole used to be-- I don't know how they are now-- but they used to be a very politically debating people all around.
You know, they were always talking about politics.
It's not like in Canada now.
Maybe we are talking more politics here than we ever did, but before when I came to Canada, politics wasn't a very big subject here-- which I loved, by the way.
But it was-- the, the nervousness started to show up.
And people we have known have been-- and had the chance to emigrate to wherever they could , they did.
My father wasn't well.
He was an invalid-- not in phys-- like not a visible invalid when he was dressed, but he was not acceptable to any country in the world as an immigrant.
And therefore, in our case, that was out of the question because in 19-- now we are going to-- coming to the time when the Germans came to, to Czechoslovakia in 1939.
And we knew-- like we.
I'm saying we, but I'm really saying my mother and father because my sister and me, we really didn't have anything much to say about this.
But the decision was that the family is not going to get split because there were children who were being sent to England in states and everywhere.
But my parents decided that whatever is coming, we are staying together on the account of we are Czech.
It's going to be bad, but it'll blow over.
Nobody in the world could have imagined what is going to happen, and nobody did.
And they felt that they didn't want us children to be sent God knows where, and so there we stayed together.
That was a decision of theirs.
Oh yes.
Well, you know, the discussion around the table-- I mean, in Europe, you don't sit in a living room in an armchair.
Maybe today you do, but they didn't in those days.
They would sit around the dining room table and discuss it up and down, whatever it was.
I mean, it wasn't anything I could understand, obviously.
But the discussions were there, and it-- everybody agreed and disagreed and this and that.
And as a child, you don't get the reasoning, and you don't get the point, but you certainly get the tones.
And I think that is what I would say would be my memory of them of saying-- of the certain uptightness.
Me?
Not really.
Not-- in 1933, it certainly did not affect me.
It didn't affect me at all.
And you know, I was in bed by the time.
I could just hear the murmurs from the dining room.
Actually, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not, not really because you see, we were not in the Jewish community and-- at that time.
So I would hear about it.
So and so-- you know, do you remember so-and-so?
They have got an uncle in wherever whatever.
And they had just got their visa, and they are going, and stuff like that.
But nobody close to me, no.
Oh, absolutely.
Well, the major change was in March '39 when all of a sudden, up the street the Germans were marching in.
Well, the first memory of the Germans coming in them was that what-- what's happening?
Look at all these soldiers.
Don't forget I was nine-- not quite nine.
And, and I knew that the grownups were very, very upset and that whatever political-- they changed it into some kind of a-- there were some kind of a change in, in the political structure.
And they called it-- I don't know what they called it anyhow.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's about all it-- you know, it was a scary feeling seeing them because the scary feeling came from all these soldiers marching in.
And I kind of sensed the scariness from the grownups.
I think that I did not comprehend what is going to happen or what it is all about in that sense.
War wasn't spoken of.
The Sudetenland-- oh yes.
There was one family which I did know about who went to the States, and that was a family who owned a textile shop where my father worked.
And they closed down, and they went to the States.
They had great chalet just a few streets away.
There was a part of that Prague where you had like housing, you know, with a garden and whatnot.
And they were-- they were rich.
And they, whatever they did, they got a visa for the whole family, and they went.
And my father had to-- that was-- I'm going back.
This is before the Germans came.
And my father had to get a different job, which he did right away because he was well-known in his profession.
But that was already with the-- with the Sudetenland, and it had his [INAUDIBLE].
And that is actually-- going back to your question-- that is when we-- I realized that there is something wrong.
We, we are not just Czechs.
We are Jewish too.
Because there were some stipulations, and my mother and father were discussing them between them.
And I would catch odd sentences which I would understand.
And the stipulations were-- well, the biggest stipulation was as soon as the Germans came six months later, my father lost the job.
So that was the stipulation because he was-- the people whom he worked for, as long as I could remember, they were already in the States.
I think so.
I think so.
But I couldn't-- I think so.
Oh no.
Not at that time.
Not at the time.
That-- he just took whatever his know-how was to that new firm because he wasn't discriminating upon at that particular time.
Right.
It continued.
Pretty much I was going to school, though, you know, people were now different.
How different, I cannot tell you, but they were different.
And I cont-- now that is '39.
I was in grade three.
And it-- like or-- the live-in, who was like part of the family, she had to go because my father's salary must have gone down.
And well, I mean, it wasn't such a big thing, but, you know, that was the first.
And after that, I went to grade four, and in grade four, they forbade the, the Jewish children to go to a normal school.
There was one Jewish school in Prague, which of course I didn't get into.
So I got one year private tutoring from a student who was Jewish and very nice and very patient and was paid for it by my father.
And but then we couldn't-- I obviously could-- my father couldn't afford to continue in that style, and that was that.
His health, health problems were internal, and he was classified like for non-immigration.
I imagine that even today with that particular illness-- I mean, he was perfectly fit with a certain problems which could be seen to under normal circumstances.
And then we found out that in Theresienstadt, even not under normal circumstances, he saw to the problems he had.
But it-- he was officially unacceptable to any country for immigration.
Right.
